schedule.title: SNC1W Semester 2 (2025-2026)
schedule.overview: Based on the course calendar, Semester 2 runs from February 2, 2026 to the June 2026 exam period and moves through Inquiry, Chemistry, Space, Electricity, and Biology/Ecology.

currentExamRange.subject: Chemistry
currentExamRange.note: Content posted on or after March 23, 2026 is part of the next Chemistry test range.

units.name: Inquiry
units.notes: Scientific questioning, controlled experiments, metric system, graphing, lab reports, and measurement investigations.

units.name: Chemistry
units.notes: Classification of matter, particle theory, physical and chemical change, periodic table, metals and non-metals, gas tests, atomic structure, bonding, and chemical formulas.

units.name: Space
units.notes: Earth-Moon-Sun interactions, eclipses, northern lights, spectroscopy, star life cycles, big bang theory, and space exploration.

units.name: Electricity
units.notes: Static electricity, charging, circuit diagrams, series and parallel circuits, current, voltage, resistance, and Ohm's law.

units.name: Biology / Ecology
units.notes: Energy flow, cycling of matter, biodiversity, climate and food security, and human effects on ecosystems.

keyDates.label: Inquiry unit test and rotocopter lab report due
keyDates.note: Inquiry assessment checkpoint before Chemistry begins.

keyDates.label: Chemistry quiz
keyDates.note: Includes learning up to and including physical and chemical change.

keyDates.label: Metals and non-metals lab analysis due in class
keyDates.note: Comes right before gas test prep and atomic theory work.

keyDates.label: Chemistry unit test
keyDates.note: Scheduled just before the Space unit starts on April 14, 2026.

keyDates.label: Space unit test
keyDates.note: Wraps the Space unit and begins the Electricity unit the same day.

keyDates.label: Electricity quiz: static electricity
keyDates.note: Mid-unit checkpoint during current electricity and circuits.

keyDates.label: Electricity unit test
keyDates.note: Final electricity assessment before Biology / Ecology starts.

keyDates.label: Ecology unit test
keyDates.note: Final unit test before review days and exam week.

keyDates.label: Exam period
keyDates.note: Block exam days begin June 18, 2026 and continue through June 24, 2026.

upcomingSupport.summary: This card explains why hydrogen is written as 1.008 on the periodic table and how average atomic mass is different from mass number.
beforeClass: Atomic mass uses the unit u, which means atomic mass unit.
u is a very small unit used for atoms; it is not the same as grams.
Hydrogen on the periodic table is written as 1.008 u because it is an average, not one exact atom.
Most hydrogen atoms are Hydrogen-1, so the average stays very close to 1.
quickQuestions: Why is hydrogen written as 1.008 instead of 1?
What unit is used for atomic mass?
Why is average atomic mass often a decimal?
How is mass number different from average atomic mass?
Why does Hydrogen-1 affect the average more than Hydrogen-2 or Hydrogen-3?
teacherQuestions: What does the 1.008 beside hydrogen mean?
Why is the periodic table value a decimal instead of a whole number?
Explain the difference between hydrogen's mass number and hydrogen's average atomic mass.
flexibleThinkingExamples: Hydrogen-1 is the most common isotope: 1 proton, 0 neutrons.
Hydrogen-2 is much less common: 1 proton, 1 neutron. It is also called deuterium.
Hydrogen-3 is much rarer: 1 proton, 2 neutrons.
Because most hydrogen atoms are Hydrogen-1, the weighted average stays near 1 and becomes 1.008 u.

upcomingSupport.summary: Before class, learn how simple lab tests help identify common gases such as oxygen, hydrogen, and carbon dioxide.
beforeClass: Know that different gases can be identified with different test results.
Be ready to connect each gas to a specific observation in the lab.
Preview the safety idea that tests are done carefully with small samples.
Be ready to record detailed observations such as ignition, glowing splint result, colour, and visible changes.
quickQuestions: Which gas relights a glowing splint?
Which gas makes a small squeaky pop with a burning splint?
Which gas in this lab is linked to fire extinguishers?
Which gas helps support breathing in hospitals?
How can you connect a gas property to a real-life use?

resources.label: YouTube: Physics Teacher - Do you know your Gas Tests from Grade 9 science?

labSteps: Hydrogen: hydrochloric acid + magnesium ribbon, then test with a burning splint.
Oxygen: hydrogen peroxide + potassium iodide, then test with a glowing splint.
Carbon dioxide: vinegar + baking soda, then test with a burning splint and, if needed, a glowing splint.
propertyUseLinks: Hydrogen is flammable, so it can be used as a fuel source such as rocket fuel or other energy applications.
Oxygen supports combustion, so it is useful for medical breathing support and processes that need burning.
Carbon dioxide does not support combustion, so it is used in fire extinguishers.
teacherQuestions: How are the physical properties of the gases similar?
How are the physical properties different?
How are the chemical properties different?
Which gas is used in fire extinguishers and why?
Which gas was used in balloons and why is it no longer commonly used?
Which gas is used to help hospital patients breathe?
Pick one property and explain why it makes that substance useful in real life.
Explain why a material's physical property can make it a good choice for a job, like carbon in pencil lead.
flexibleThinkingExamples: Non-metal carbon in pencil lead is soft enough to rub onto paper, so that physical property makes it useful for writing.
A teacher may ask for a property-to-use explanation, not just the name of the gas or substance.
Practice answering in this format: property -> what it means -> why that helps in the real-world use.

topics.summary: The planetary model explains that electrons move in fixed energy levels around the nucleus, can jump between orbits, and release light when they fall to lower energy levels.
keyConcepts: The nuclear model could not explain where electrons were located.
Scientists thought moving electrons should release radiation and crash into the nucleus, but atoms do not collapse like that.
Bohr proposed a planetary model in which electrons orbit the nucleus like planets orbit the Sun.
Each electron orbit has a definite amount of energy.
Electrons farther from the nucleus have greater energy.
Electrons cannot exist between orbits but can jump from one orbit to another.
When electrons drop from higher to lower orbits, they release energy as light.
Each orbit can hold a maximum number of electrons.
teacherNotes: The line spectra idea is the evidence that pushes the lesson beyond the old nuclear model.
Teacher quiz questions may ask why the nuclear model was incomplete before the planetary model was proposed.
Know both the strengths and the limitation: the planetary model still could not explain line spectra for atoms with two or more electrons.
quizEasy: Why was the nuclear model incomplete?
What happens when an electron moves from a higher orbit to a lower orbit?
How does electron energy change as distance from the nucleus increases?
quizMedium: Explain why scientists thought the old nuclear model should make atoms unstable.
What evidence from line spectra supported the idea of fixed energy levels?
quizHard: Explain one strength and one limitation of the planetary model.
Why did scientists later need quantum mechanics even after the planetary model?
feynmanChecklist: I can explain why the nuclear model needed to be revised.
I can describe fixed energy levels in simple English.
I can explain how line spectra connect to electron jumps.
applications: Use the orbit and energy-level ideas when explaining emission spectra and why atoms produce specific colours of light.

topics.summary: Atomic structure focuses on the three subatomic particles, atomic number, mass number, isotopes, and how to calculate protons, neutrons, and electrons in a neutral atom.
keyConcepts: Protons are positive and neutrons are neutral in the nucleus; electrons are negative and move around the nucleus.
Atomic number = number of protons, and in a neutral atom it also equals the number of electrons.
Mass number = protons + neutrons.
Neutrons = mass number - protons.
Isotopes are atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
Atomic mass on the periodic table is a weighted average, not the same as the mass number.
teacherNotes: The slide breaks atomic structure into particle identity first, then atomic number, mass number, isotopes, atomic mass, and finally the reminder formulas.
Be ready for teacher questions that ask for the difference between atomic number, mass number, and atomic mass.
The practice sheet focuses on identifying particles, neutral charge, and filling in proton-electron-neutron tables.
quizEasy: Which particle decides the atomic number of an atom?
In a neutral atom, how are protons and electrons related?
quizMedium: Explain the difference between mass number and atomic mass.
Why can two isotopes belong to the same element even though their masses differ?
If an atom has atomic number 8 and mass number 16, how many neutrons does it have?
quizHard: Use the slide formulas to explain how you would find protons, electrons, and neutrons for a neutral atom.
Why is the decimal on the periodic table not the same as the mass number shown in isotope notation?
feynmanChecklist: I can explain atomic number, mass number, and atomic mass in simple words.
I can tell someone how to find neutrons using mass number and protons.
I can explain why isotopes are the same element but not exactly the same atom.
applications: Use atomic number, mass number, and neutral charge rules to complete particle tables quickly on worksheets and quizzes.
Watch for teacher questions that compare isotope notation with the decimal atomic mass on the periodic table.

topics.summary: The current Chemistry test range starts with the March 23, 2026 content and continues through the newest class materials, including atomic structure and Bohr-Rutherford work.
keyConcepts: Periodic table patterns
The periodic table
Atomic theory
Atomic structure
Bohr-Rutherford diagrams and the planetary model
Coding for Science connections where assigned
teacherNotes: March 23, 2026 and later Chemistry posts are part of the next test range.
Use recent Google Classroom materials as the main review source.
Atomic Structure is a priority review area because a quiz is expected next week.
feynmanChecklist: I can explain the main periodic table patterns.
I can describe the key idea of atomic theory in simple words.
